one_line_takeaway: In a live 24-hour CTF, autonomous Claude-based agents (best: proprietary agent + Sonnet-4.5, 4900 pts, ~4hrs, ~$96) outperformed most human-AI teams, while human collaborators were mainly held back by their own prompting and context-specification quality rather than by model capability -- and a small dose of human intervention ('pair hacking') pushed the best agent to first place overall.
key_finding: Participants' trust and expected AI effectiveness dropped after hands-on use (expected solve counts fell by delta 0.47, F1), and human bottlenecks -- not model reasoning limits -- constrained solve rates: high-AI-expertise users had 66% prompt-engineering-strategy prevalence and 62% episode success rate versus 23% prevalence / 27% success for low-expertise users (F7), with 42% vs 17% AI-error prevalence for novice vs expert AI users and 91% of guardrail-refusal errors occurring in novice logs. Delegation (38% prevalence) dominated over collaborative refinement/confirmation-seeking (~16% average), despite 80% of participants predicting trial-and-error as their primary mode pre-competition (F6). On the agent side, the strongest configuration (proprietary agent + Sonnet-4.5) reached 4900 points -- second place among top-10 human teams -- in about 4 hours of cumulative runtime at $95.32-$96.32 in API cost (F11); Claude Code+Sonnet-4.5 scored 4600 and proprietary+Opus-4.1 scored 4300. Sonnet-4.5 gave the highest mean success rate across the four agent frameworks (48.53%), versus 35.29% for Opus-4.1 and only 14.71% for Haiku-3.5, with the proprietary agent collapsing from 4900 (Sonnet-4.5) to 1100 points (Haiku-3.5), showing backbone-model capability sets a hard ceiling that framework/tooling design cannot compensate for (F13). Agents beat humans in crypto (75% vs 51.16% solved, delta +23.84%) and on a specific logic challenge O3 (about 92% vs 44.19%, delta +51.16%), and in forensics/'other' categories, but underperformed humans in reverse engineering (delta -10.47%) and struggled on web exploitation due to limited interactive-session tool support (F14). A human-in-the-loop 'pair hacking' intervention on 5 previously-failed challenges recovered 2 additional solves, raising the proprietary agent's score to 5700 and taking first place on both leaderboards (F15), illustrated by a case study where a human broke an execution-fix retry loop, supplied a blocked rockyou.txt dictionary file, and redirected the agent to inspect decryption logic requiring replication of thousands of random() calls.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first empirical study of human-AI collaboration in a live, onsite Capture-the-Flag (CTF) competition. The authors (i) ran pre/post surveys with 41 participants (of 95 total competitors) at a 24-hour, university-level CTF with 17 newly authored challenges (7700 total points across easy/medium/hard tiers), (ii) built and deployed an instrumented AI assistant, CTFriend (giving participants access to Claude Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.1, and Haiku 3.5 via a Streamlit UI + MCP tool layer + RAG CTF knowledge base), and qualitatively coded 2,299 chat messages across 168 logs from 38 participants using a hybrid inductive-deductive codebook (9,312 code occurrences across 72 codes/subcodes, single trained coder with a 21-day washout retest yielding Positive Agreement=0.74, median Jaccard=0.79), and (iii) in parallel benchmarked four autonomous CTF agent frameworks (a proprietary security assistant, Claude Code, the NYU CTFBench agent, and Cybench) each paired with three Claude-family models (12 configurations total) on the identical fresh challenge set, comparing agent scoreboards against the top-10 human teams.
threat_model: Not an attack/defense threat model in the traditional sense; the paper studies human-AI collaboration dynamics during offensive security tasks (CTF challenge solving spanning forensics, cryptography, reverse engineering, web exploitation, and misc/OSINT/hardware categories). The 'adversary' framing is CTF challenge design itself (simulated vulnerabilities), not a security attacker targeting the study's system. Risks discussed are collaboration/process risks (over-delegation, hallucination, guardrail-triggered refusals, 'answer shopping') rather than a formal attacker-capability threat model.
limitations: Single-site, single-event study (N=41 surveyed of 95 total competitors) limiting statistical generalizability -- authors explicitly claim analytic generalization/transferability rather than statistical generalization. Individual score attribution may be confounded by team effort (self-reported scores manually cross-referenced but risk remains). Qualitative coding was performed by a single trained coder (mitigated with an exploration-confirmation codebook process and a 21-day-washout test-retest showing Positive Agreement=0.74, median Jaccard=0.79, IQR=0.17), so idiosyncratic-coder and residual construct-validity risk remain per the authors' own Threats to Validity section. Log corpus had uneven per-participant coverage (variance in number of logs and tasks per participant), only partially mitigated by episode/turn normalization. Findings are tied to a specific system configuration (CTFriend + specific Claude model versions + specific agent frameworks) and the authors note LLM behavior shifts across versions/interfaces/tool configurations, so observed patterns may not generalize over time.

datasets_used: Custom 17-challenge, 5-category (forensics, cryptography, reverse engineering, web exploitation, other) CTF benchmark authored specifically for this event (7700 total points; easy=300/medium=500/hard=1000 point tiers), converted into a JSON-schema machine-readable format for agent evaluation
Pre-/post-CTF survey data from 41 participants (of 95 total competitors)
CTFriend interaction log corpus: 2,299 chat messages across 168 logs from 38 participants, coded into 9,312 occurrences of 72 codes/subcodes
quantitative_results: Expected AI solve-count dropped by delta 0.47 after hands-on use (F1)
44% of participants still planned to use AI in future security work post-competition

High-AI-expertise users: 66% prompt-engineering-strategy prevalence, 62% episode success rate, 17% AI-error prevalence vs low-expertise users: 23% prevalence, 27% success rate, 42% error prevalence; 91% of guardrail-refusal errors occurred in novice-AI-user logs
Delegation pattern prevalence 38% vs collaborative-refinement/confirmation-seeking/rejection averaging ~16%, despite 80% of participants pre-reporting trial-and-error as expected primary strategy
Median latency of 23s between AI code/command suggestion and user execution; <1% command/code rejection rate after review; 14s median gap between AI 'next steps' suggestions and user simple approval

Repeated full-challenge delegation (Del2) occurred in 23% of lowest-scoring/lowest-domain-knowledge teams' episodes, with a lower success rate (Del2Fail=13%) than the overall novice success rate (27%)
Best agent configuration (proprietary agent + Sonnet-4.5): 4900 points, ~4 hours cumulative runtime, $95.32-$96.32 API cost, second place among top-10 human teams
Claude Code + Sonnet-4.5: 4600 points (would rank third); proprietary agent + Opus-4.1: 4300 points (would rank fifth) among top-10 human teams
Mean success rate by model across the 4 agent frameworks: Sonnet-4.5 48.53%, Opus-4.1 35.29%, Haiku-3.5 14.71%
Proprietary agent score collapse across models: 4900 (Sonnet-4.5) -> 1100 (Haiku-3.5); NYU agent 1100 and Cybench 300 under Haiku-3.5

Per-category agent vs human solved-rate deltas: crypto 75% (agents) vs 51.16% (humans), delta +23.84%; logic challenge O3 ~92% vs 44.19%, delta +51.16%; forensics delta +10.47% (agents higher); reverse engineering delta -10.47% (humans higher, i.e. agents lower); 'other' category delta +55.62% (agents higher)

Table 2 per-model/per-agent overall success rates: Sonnet-4.5 -- Proprietary Agent 12/17 (70.59%), Claude Code 11/17 (64.71%), NYU Agent 7/17 (41.18%), Cybench 3/17 (17.65%); Opus-4.1 -- Proprietary Agent 10/17 (58.82%), Claude Code 8/17 (47.06%), NYU Agent 4/17 (23.53%), Cybench 2/17 (11.76%); Haiku-3.5 -- Proprietary Agent 3/17 (17.65%), Claude Code 3/17 (17.65%), NYU Agent 3/17 (17.65%), Cybench 1/17 (5.88%)

Pair-hacking intervention on 5 previously-failed challenges recovered 2 additional solves, raising the proprietary agent's final score to 5700 points (first place on both human and agent leaderboards)
baselines_compared: Four autonomous agent frameworks: a proprietary security assistant (top performer at Hack The Box's Neurogrid AI-only CTF), Claude Code (general coding assistant), the NYU CTFBench agent, and Cybench
Three backbone models per agent framework: Claude Sonnet-4.5, Opus-4.1, Haiku-3.5 (12 total agent configurations)
Top-10 human competition teams (drawn from the live 24-hour, 95-participant CTF) used as the human performance baseline for agent comparison
